findings.title: unsafe block without SAFETY comment
findings.description: Unsafe blocks should document why they're safe. Add a `// SAFETY:` comment.
findings.why_it_matters: Unsafe code bypasses Rust's safety guarantees. SAFETY comments are essential for code review.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 4 functions in call cycle (complexity 91)
findings.description: Mutual recursion detected: 4 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/clap/clap_complete/src/engine/complete.rs::complete:13 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/clap/clap_complete/src/engine/complete.rs::complete_arg:135 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/clap/clap_complete/src/engine/complete.rs::complete_arg_value:326 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/clap/clap_complete/src/engine/complete.rs::complete_option:228. Aggregate complexity: 91.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Structural bridge: `clap_complete/src/lib.rs` separates components of [3175, 32]
findings.description: `clap_complete/src/lib.rs` is a structural bridge. Removing it would split the graph into components of sizes [3175, 32]. All communication between these components currently flows through this single node.
findings.suggested_fix: Reduce coupling through this node by introducing alternative call paths, extracting shared interfaces, or splitting the bridge function into independently accessible units.
findings.why_it_matters: A structural bridge is a single node whose failure disconnects entire subsystems. This creates fragile architecture where a bug, API change, or refactor in one function can cascade to all dependent components.

findings.title: unsafe block without SAFETY comment
findings.description: Unsafe blocks should document why they're safe. Add a `// SAFETY:` comment.
findings.why_it_matters: Unsafe code bypasses Rust's safety guarantees. SAFETY comments are essential for code review.

Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/clap/clap_builder/src/builder/command.rs::impl<Command>::_build_recursive:4389 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/clap/clap_builder/src/builder/command.rs::impl<Command>::_build_self:4396 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/clap/clap_builder/src/builder/command.rs::impl<Command>::_check_help_and_version:4802 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/clap/clap_builder/src/builder/command.rs::impl<Command>::build:4384 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/clap/tests/macros.rs::long_help:138. Aggregate complexity: 39.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Architectural Bottleneck: _build (utility function - expected high fan-in)
findings.description:
Function '_build' is called by 31 functions and has complexity 17. Changes here are high-risk.

Betweenness centrality: 0.0061 - this function sits on many execution paths between other functions.

**Note:** This function was identified as a utility. High fan-in is expected, but the high complexity may still warrant attention.

📊 Threshold: 20 (adaptive) | Actual: 17 | Default would be: 20 | Profile: p90=5, p95=9, mean=2.6, n=1914
findings.suggested_fix: Consider splitting this utility into smaller, focused helpers. High complexity in shared utilities is risky.
findings.why_it_matters: Bottlenecks are single points of failure that amplify bugs. High fan-in with high complexity means many callers depend on risky code.

findings.title: Panic risk: unwrap() without context
findings.description: Using `unwrap()` can cause panics. Consider `?`, `unwrap_or`, or proper error handling.
findings.why_it_matters: Panics crash the program without recovery. Using proper error handling makes code more robust and debuggable.

findings.title: High panic density in `assert_app`: 4 panic-family calls
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Function `assert_app` contains 4 calls to `.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, or `panic!()` at line 99, line 110, line 125, line 142. This makes the function fragile and likely to panic at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix:
Reduce panic points by:
- Using `?` operator for error propagation
- Using `unwrap_or` / `unwrap_or_default` / `unwrap_or_else`
- Returning `Result<T, E>` instead of panicking
- Extracting fallible logic into a helper that returns Result
findings.why_it_matters: Functions with many panic points are fragile. A single unexpected None/Err will crash the program. Consolidating error handling makes code more robust and easier to reason about.

findings.title: Long method: push_attrs (476 lines)
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Function 'push_attrs' has 476 lines (effective threshold: 150).

**Graph Analysis:**
Orchestrator pattern: calls 15 functions (reduced severity)
findings.suggested_fix:
This appears to be an orchestrator function (coordinates many calls).
If it must remain long, ensure it:
1. Has clear section comments
2. Handles errors at each step
3. Has a clear flow (consider a state machine for complex flows)
findings.why_it_matters: Long methods are hard to understand, test, and maintain. Each function should do one thing well.

findings.title: Mutex poisoning risk
findings.description: Using .lock().unwrap() will panic if the mutex is poisoned. Consider handling the PoisonError or using parking_lot::Mutex.
findings.suggested_fix:
Handle poisoning: `mutex.lock().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner())`
Or use `parking_lot::Mutex` which has no poisoning.
findings.why_it_matters: Poisoned mutexes cause cascading panics across threads.

findings.title: Mutex poisoning risk

findings.suggested_fix:

Or use `parking_lot::Mutex` which has no poisoning.
findings.why_it_matters: Poisoned mutexes cause cascading panics across threads.

- Extracting fallible logic into a helper that returns Result
findings.why_it_matters: Functions with many panic points are fragile. A single unexpected None/Err will crash the program. Consolidating error handling makes code more robust and easier to reason about.

findings.title: Structural bridge: `/tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/clap/tests/derive/value_enum.rs::default_value:42` separates components of [3187, 20]
findings.description: `/tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/clap/tests/derive/value_enum.rs::default_value:42` is a structural bridge. Removing it would split the graph into components of sizes [3187, 20]. All communication between these components currently flows through this single node.
findings.suggested_fix: Reduce coupling through this node by introducing alternative call paths, extracting shared interfaces, or splitting the bridge function into independently accessible units.
findings.why_it_matters: A structural bridge is a single node whose failure disconnects entire subsystems. This creates fragile architecture where a bug, API change, or refactor in one function can cascade to all dependent components.

findings.title: High file-level panic density: 13 panic-family calls
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] File `clap_builder/src/builder/debug_asserts.rs` contains 13 total panic-family calls (`.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, `panic!()`) outside of test code. Consider refactoring to reduce panic surface.
findings.suggested_fix:
Consider:
- Introducing a module-level error type
- Replacing unwrap chains with `?` propagation
- Using `anyhow::Context` for better error messages
findings.why_it_matters: Files with many panic points are hard to maintain and make the overall application fragile. Centralizing error handling improves reliability.

findings.title: Excessive nesting: 9 levels in push_attrs
findings.description:
9 levels of nesting (threshold: 7).

**Analysis:**
In function: `push_attrs`
High complexity: 92 (nesting compounds this)
Contains 1 match/switch level(s) (discounted from raw depth 10)
Existing helpers that could reduce nesting: assert_attr_kind, from_lit, attribute

Threshold: 5 (adaptive) | Actual: 9 | Default would be: 5 | Profile: p90=2, p95=3, mean=0.8, n=1914
findings.suggested_fix:
This function already has helpers like `assert_attr_kind`. Consider:
1. Extract more nested blocks into similar helpers
2. Use guard clauses (early returns) to reduce nesting
3. Replace nested ifs with switch/match
findings.why_it_matters: Deep nesting makes code hard to read and maintain. Each level increases cognitive load exponentially.
